old_claim.note: Stock Ticker Systems provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Stock ticker systems are parallel financial-information terminals and clients, not a prerequisite for news agencies that existed before ticker systems.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica's history section places Havas in 1835, AP in 1846, Wolff in 1849, and Reuters in 1851, with agencies serving newspapers through telegraphed text.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source establishes newspaper and telegraph-based news agencies before stock ticker systems became available.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This refutes the stock-ticker prerequisite because ticker systems are later financial terminals, while the agency model already existed around newspapers and telegraph services.

ontology_before: The node implied that stock ticker systems enabled transoceanic news agencies.
ontology_after: The node is scoped to newspaper-facing wire agencies over telegraph and cable networks, with stock tickers excluded as parallel financial display systems.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: transoceanic_news_agencies no longer directly depends on stock_ticker_systems.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to financial quote-wire terminal services rather than news agencies.
A stronger source showed stock ticker systems were a prerequisite for Reuters/Havas/AP-style agency operation.
Stock ticker systems were rescoped to mean all telegraphed financial news rather than ticker display infrastructure.
short_reason: News agencies predate stock tickers and did not require ticker terminals.
